data.title: VB: Small Caps Join The Breakout Party
data.description:
Small caps loudly joined the S&P 500 at a fresh all-time high following encouraging peace-talk developments between the United States and Iran. On Friday, April 17, the Vanguard Small-Cap ETF (VB) reached a new record, finally eclipsing its February 20, 2026, peak. Zoom out, and we find that VB trails only international equities among the S&P 500, mid-caps, and the Vanguard FTSE All-World ex-US ETF (VEU).

I was bullish on VB back in November 2025, and shares have returned 15% since then, dividends included. That’s 9 percentage points of alpha to the S&P 500. Today, despite a torrid rally off the March low, I reiterate a "Buy" rating. Amid increasing earnings estimates and the temperature coming down on the geopolitical front, I expect this week’s breakout to be durable.

Small Caps Leading Among U.S. Sizes YoY

Small Caps: Higher Yield, Bigger Buyback Percentage Compared To S&P 500

Small-Cap EPS Forecasts Rising

According to the issuer, VB seeks to track the performance of the CRSP US Small Cap Index, which measures the investment return of small-capitalization stocks and offers investors a convenient way to match the performance of a diversified group of small companies. The ETF employs a passively managed, full-replication approach.

VB is a large ETF, with more than $70 billion in assets under management as of April 16, 2026. Its annual expense ratio is low at only 3 basis points, while the trailing 12-month dividend yield is about on par with that of the S&P 500 at 1.25%.

Share-price momentum is strong right now, earning VB a solid B+ ETF Grade in that category by Seeking Alpha’s quantitative scoring system. In terms of risk, VB scores well, thanks to its somewhat modest standard deviation percentage (the conflict in the Middle East, notwithstanding) and in-check annual volatility level. At the moment, implied volatility has dipped to 19%, which is the lowest since late February.

On liquidity, VB also scores well. Average daily volume is north of 800,000 shares, while the median 30-day bid/ask spread is tight at 3 basis points, per Vanguard.

For an update on the portfolio, the 4-star, Gold-rated ETF by Morningstar has more exposure across the style box than prospective investors may realize. Indeed, more than one-third of the allocation is classified as mid-cap, which is key for factor and style managers to consider. The breakout among value, blend, and growth is about even, which is not too much changed since my previous assessment.

On valuation, VB remains a bargain, in my view. The current price-to-earnings ratio is a bit more than 16x, while the long-term EPS growth rate is formidable at 11.36%. The resulting PEG ratio is attractive at under 1.5.x

VB: Portfolio & Factor Profiles

Zooming into the sector breakout, Industrials is the largest component, but it’s only 21% of the product. To go along with that cyclical segment, the growth-centered Information Technology sector is runner-up at 14.2%. Elsewhere, value and defensives enter the scene just before I.T., with Financials and Healthcare rounding out the top 4.

I’d note that among the 11 S&P 500 sector ETFs, only Information Technology (XLK) and Real Estate (XLRE) are notching 52-week highs.

VB: Holdings & Dividend Information

Turning to the calendar, VB has historically performed very well from April through July. So, if the past 10 years of price data holds, the rally that began in late March could have legs well into the third quarter.

VB: Bullish Calendar Trends Through July

The Technical Take

With a compelling valuation, diversified portfolio, and bullish seasonals, VB’s technical situation is very strong. Notice in the chart below that the small-cap ETF has launched to a new all-time high, joining the S&P 500 at a record level. The RSI momentum oscillator at the top of the graph is likewise tagging its best mark going back to November of 2024. Couple that with a rising long-term 200-day moving average, and it’s clear that the bulls control the primary trend.

Also take a look at the volume by price profile on the left side of the graph. There’s actually a surprisingly high number of shares traded just below the February peak. That tells me that we could see a solid support zone if we see a pullback (although a false breakout would not be an encouraging feature).

In the here and now, I calculate a measured move upside price target to be about $305, based on the depth of the February to March decline and subsequent breakout today. On the downside, a gap lingers at $265, and there’s now a smaller gap just under $280 from the up-move on the day of this writing. While gaps often get filled, not all do. A case in point was one from just about a year ago, after Liberation Day.

VB: Breakout Above the February High, Rising 200dma, Strong RSI Momentum Confirmation

The Bottom Line

I have a "Buy" rating on VB. The small-cap index ETF appears to be a solid value, given earnings growth trends and the current P/E. Technical price action is very strong, especially with today’s breakout and sanguine seasonal trends.

data.title: Low-Stress 8% Yields I Would Bet My Retirement On
data.description:
While there are plenty of high-yielding stocks out there, there are very few that can be counted on through thick and thin to continue paying at their current level. Therefore, it can be a difficult environment for retirees who want to significantly juice their retirement income without going far out on the risk spectrum.

In this article, I'm going to detail two exceptions to this rule: one which is an equity that yields 8.2% on the next 12-month basis and is expected to continue growing its distribution at an inflation-beating pace for the foreseeable future, and the other one is a broadly diversified ETF that pays out monthly dividends and yields about 8% on an annualized basis.

An Actively Managed Bond ETF Worth Owning

The first opportunity to discuss is an actively managed bond fund called the Infrastructure Capital Bond ETF (BNDS). BNDS implements an actively managed high-conviction bond strategy while giving investors a simple, liquid ETF that they can use to access it easily. Rather than tracking a cap-weighted index of heavily covered issuers like many of its peers, such as the SPDR Bloomberg High Yield Bond ETF (JNK) and the iShares iBoxx $ High Yield Corporate Bond ETF (HYG), BNDS instead scours the fixed income space for mispriced credits, with a particular focus on real asset-intensive sectors like energy pipelines, mortgage rates, and real estate businesses.

For example, they purchased Plains All American Pipeline (PAA)(PAGP) bonds at an 8.5% yield, which was an incredible bargain for them. Given that PAA has a solid balance sheet, strong tangible asset collateral, and long-duration fee-based contracts, that yield was highly sustainable, and the bond investment had to have a very low risk of loss over the long term. BNDS looks for other opportunities like this that it can successfully arbitrage, and then regularly recycles capital to help boost its income per share over time while also delivering total return outperformance.

Overall, BNDS has 76 positions as of the end of March, with its top ten holdings making up about 40% of the portfolio. This helps it balance high-conviction positions to generate outperformance potential while still being diversified to make it a low-risk aggregate investment for investors, putting it in a similar category to high-yield bonds in terms of risk while delivering even better income and total return potential.

Growing Dividends From A Bond Fund

The ETF not only pays out an attractive dividend yield of around 8% annualized, but it has actually grown its dividend slightly since its inception back in 2025. This reflects its successful ability to recycle out of positions that appreciate in value and into undervalued positions, as normally bond funds do not generate any dividend growth in and of themselves since their underlying holdings are fixed income in nature. BNDS also does not use any leverage, which keeps its volatility low and puts it on a very sustainable footing even in uncertain market environments.

While its 0.81% expense ratio is fairly high compared to some of its peers, it has outperformed its peers substantially since it launched, therefore fully justifying its higher expense ratio that is used for implementing its active management strategy.

Risks To Keep In Mind

While I think that the fund should be able to pay sustainable and potentially even slightly growing dividends over time, making it an attractive and fairly low-stress income vehicle for retirement, it is still worth noting that BNDS invests primarily in below-investment-grade bonds. While it is diversified, there's still a very real risk of loss should the economy enter a severe economic downturn and/or if the management team makes a poor decision in terms of their bond selection.

Overall, though, the focus on real asset sectors, with about 40% concentration in energy and real estate, helps to mitigate risks, as does the active management, which helps to spot problematic securities early and mitigate those risks to help support the long-term capital preservation and income generation goals of the fund. It is also worth knowing that rising long-term interest rates would also push the NAV lower, even if the underlying credits remain sound.

An 8.2%-Yielder With Serious Growth Potential

The other high-yield opportunity that I like a lot right now is a midstream MLP (AMLP) that issues a K-1 by the name of MPLX LP (MPLX). MPLX is majority owned by Marathon Petroleum (MPC) and operates in two core segments:

  • The crude and refined products and logistics segment, which makes up about 65% of its EBITDA

  • Its natural gas and energy services segment, which makes up about 35% of its EBITDA

MPLX is largely commodity-price resistant as it generates most of its cash flows in this segment from fixed-rate contracts, minimum volume commitments, and inflation-linked escalators, which enabled it to support a very high and growing distribution. Additionally, it is leaning heavily into its natural gas and energy services segment, as that is where significant growth potential is available, as evidenced by its 90% of growth capex this year being allocated toward that segment.

Impressive Pipeline Of Growth Projects

Growth projects coming online in the next few years include:

  • The Secretariat 2 processing plant in the Permian Basin

  • The Titan sour gas treatment complex

  • The BANGL pipeline expansion

  • Harmon Creek 3

  • The LPG export terminal joint venture, which is expected to come online in 2028

Each of these projects has been underwritten at a mid-teens unlevered return and should help the company to deliver its mid-to-high single-digit EBITDA growth rate over the next few years and potentially beyond. MPLX also has a strong balance sheet with a BBB credit rating from S&P, over $2 billion in cash on hand, a distribution coverage ratio of above 1.3 times, and a leverage ratio that is well within its target range, especially once accounting for its numerous growth projects coming online in the near future.

MPLX's 8.2% next 12-month yield, when combined with management guiding for 12.5% annualized distribution growth for at least the next year or two and analyst consensus estimates that have the distribution growing at a 10.6% CAGR through 2028, means that it is one of the most attractive combined current income and distribution growth opportunities in the market today.

Why Risks Are Manageable

Of course, no investment is risk-free, and MPLX is no exception, as it is largely beholden to MPC since it generates a lot of its cash flow from MPC as a counterpart, and MPC is a major unit holder of MPLX. That being said, it is a symbiotic relationship, as MPC generates a lot of cash flow from MPLX, and thus far, MPLX's phenomenal track record indicates MPC is running it fairly well.

Additionally, MPLX is leaning aggressively into growth projects, which, if they fail to deliver the return management expects, could result in materially slower distribution growth for the MLP moving forward. Of course, operating in the energy sector can also see volatility in its unit price with energy price fluctuations, though its underlying cash flows tend to be much more resilient overall.

The Retirement Portfolio Combo That Could Let You Sleep at Night

I think these two investment opportunities can combine well in a retirement portfolio because BNDS provides fixed income every month from a diversified portfolio of bonds, while MPLX is an equity play based on essential infrastructure that also has inflation resistance built into it. Combined, they deliver income north of 8% that should grow at a rate that, in aggregate, grows roughly at the rate of inflation — slightly even faster depending on weightings between these two positions, making them great additions to a low-stress income-focused retirement portfolio that investors can bet on while sleeping well at night. This is why I hold both of them in my 7–8% yielding total return outperforming and below-market-beta retirement portfolio at High Yield Investor.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Cintas: The Valuation Has Come In, But It's Not A Buy Just Yet
data.description:
Introduction

Since my initial piece on Cintas Corporation (CTAS) back in September 2024, shares of Cintas have fallen 11% while the S&P 500 has gone on to move up 27%. At the time, I recommended that new investors wait for a more attractive entry point as the valuation on Cintas looked pricey. That said, I think Cintas is a name worth revisiting as shares have lagged and the valuation multiple has compressed since the company is a high quality business with reoccurring revenues and steady margin expansion. This article will be an update to my original investment thesis where I’ll unpack what’s changed, share my expectations for Cintas in 2026, and discuss how I’m thinking about the valuation at these levels.

A look back at the recent quarter

Cintas’ core business looks healthy. In many ways, Q3’26 was pretty uneventful as the company put up consistent numbers that were largely in line with what the market had expected. On revenues, Cintas achieved a record for the quarter at $2.84 billion, which was up 8.9% compared to last year and 8.2% organically after adjusting for acquisitions.

As illustrated in the chart below, each route-based business contributed meaningfully, with First Aid and Safety Services posting 14.6% organic growth, Fire Protection Services at 10.0%, Uniform Rental and Facility Services at 7.3%, and Uniform Direct Sales at 3.1%. With strong performance across all of its segments, Cintas has been able to gain new customers, grow with existing customers, and maintaining pricing that’s been consistent with their historical 2-3% range. So in an environment where investors are potentially worried by AI disruption, conflict in the Middle East, among other concerns, Cintas is one of those steady names that has diversified exposure to resilient verticals in health care, hospitality, education, and government.

Down the income statement, Cintas’ profitability has continued to improve with gross margins expanding 40bps to 51.0% of sales. By segment, gross margins have improved across all three segments with margins of 50.3% in Uniform Rental and Facility Services, 58.1% in First Aid and Safety Services, and 50.5% in Fire Protection Services. A lot of this expansion has come from a combination of cost management and supply chain efficiencies. Further down, operating income rose 8.2% to $659.9 million, good for a 23.2% margin (up 40 basis points adjusted). With SG&A flat as a percentage of revenue, the company’s net income was $502.5 million and EPS of $1.24 was right in line with sellside estimates.

Leverage is manageable even after UniFirst acquisition

As for the balance sheet, Cintas maintains a solid credit profile with A rated debt from S&P with a stable outlook (source: Bloomberg). With debt of $2.6 billion against LTM EBITDA of $3.1 billion, leverage is less than one turn. As such, the company can access debt capital markets at low rates as evidenced by the weighted average coupon on existing debt at 4.12% (source: Bloomberg).

However, subsequent to the quarter, Cintas made a deal to acquire its competitor, UniFirst in a $5.5 billion transaction that consists of cash and stock. Paying a massive premium (over 60%) above where UniFirst was trading during its 2025 lows, Cintas had clearly had its eye on the business in the past as they tried to make a bid (unsuccessful) back in 2022.

On the balance sheet side, leverage is expected to remain modest at approximately 1.5x Debt/EBITDA following the anticipated closing of the UniFirst acquisition in 2H’26. Because of the deal, I think buybacks might be put on hold in the short term but this is likely a great deal longer term given the cost savings that the combined business will generate.

Cintas is set up well in 2026 post-UniFirst deal

Cintas’ management came out of the Q3 call optimistic and raised guidance. The new forecast projects revenue of $11.21-$11.24 billion (8.4%-8.7% total growth) and EPS of $4.86-$4.90 (10.5%-11.4% growth), excluding $0.03-$0.04 in UniFirst transaction costs.

I find this guidance reasonable and not overly optimistic, given Cintas' history of outperforming amid macro headwinds like elevated fuel costs (1.7% of revenue, with a 30% fuel spike adding just 30bps impact) and complex economic conditions. On the conference call, management noted that they’ve been consistent on pricing 2-3% higher annually and maintain high retention in the 95% range with new business/cross-sell fueling mid-to-high single-digit growth.

In terms of the UniFirst acquisition, my first impression was that this was a smart, disciplined deal that catapults Cintas to market leadership. Valued at $5.5 billion enterprise value ($310/share: $155 cash + 0.772 Cintas shares at $200.77), it implies around an 8x TTM EBITDA on their business pre-synergies. As it’s being financed via cash/credit/bridge for pro forma leverage at 1.5x, Cintas isn’t overleveraging itself to do this. Perhaps prudently and conservatively, they’re preserving buybacks/dividends post-close.

Management hosted an M&A call where they highlighted how both businesses have complementary footprints. Cintas is adding 300k new customers with the expected total reaching 1.5 million. Importantly, no revenue synergies are being modeled and instead the company is focused on $375 million in cost savings over four years from procurement, tech integration, plant efficiencies, and G&A cuts.

This seems a lot like the G&K playbook from 2017. Back then, Cintas was buying a business at a $2.2 billion enterprise value and was expecting to generate synergies of $130-$140 million in annual synergies. At a single-digit EBITDA multiple, the company’s focus on seamless integration and cutting costs made this a pretty successful deal. I see a lot of similarities between that deal and UniFirst.

Unlike the G&K deal that took four full years to achieve synergies, Cintas expects UniFirst synergies to be much faster with EPS accretion by the end of year two post-close. While 8x EBITDA isn’t cheap, it’s justified by UniFirst's quality (long-term oriented, tech catch-up underway) and Cintas' proven M&A execution. My expectation is that this should probably help boost margins 100-200 bps long-term. Even though they aren’t baking it into their expectations, there could be some cross-sell like Cintas' safety/fire to UniFirst uniforms and that could help on the revenue side. In total, the deal adds growth, enhances route density, and helps position the business well against e-commerce/hybrid threats.

As for what to watch next quarter in Q4’26, I’d expect more formal guidance for FY’27. Given that Cintas has beaten sellside estimates in all of the last eight quarters, the company is likely to beat again since Q4 EPS estimates are only a penny higher than Q3 (source: Bloomberg). On the revenue side, sales should probably be up 8% with continued organic growth of 7-8% from pricing, retention (95%+), and new wins in uniforms/facility services, plus strength in high-margin first aid (14%+ organic) and fire protection (10%). But we aren’t likely to see the benefits of UniFirst until we get to the back half of the fiscal year.

Risks

In terms of the risks, while Cintas' business model is resilient, the risk I see in the short-term is on regulatory hurdles the company might face for the UniFirst acquisition amid antitrust scrutiny from the FTC, potentially delaying close (H2 2026) or requiring divestitures. There’s a $350 million reverse termination fee on the deal where Cintas would have to pay UniFirst $350 million if regulators block the deal on antitrust grounds so that could be a potential cash outflow without a benefit to the company if the deal doesn’t close.

If and when the deal closes, there are always integration challenges post-merger. Unlike G&K or the smaller tuck-in deals that Cintas does, this is a fairly substantial deal and so there might be more tech/ERP harmonization that’s required or higher customer churn that we might see. Finally, macro and cost pressures like fuel and labor inflation could impact margins if not fully passed on through pricing.

Valuation still pricey

While I’m a fan of Cintas’ business and the deal they just announced, what holds me back is on the valuation. Even though it’s re-rated from the 27x EV/EBITDA range to 22.0x EV/EBITDA today, I think this is still a high multiple to pay, despite recognizing the quality of the earnings.

On current numbers, Cintas is trading at roughly double the valuation that they paid for UniFirst and trades at 33.4x P/E, much higher than peers at 24.6x (source: Bloomberg). Even when factoring in mid-teens EPS growth from the sellside for the next few years up to $7.57 in EPS by 2030, this is still a business that’s trading at 23.4x P/E by the end of the decade.

Conclusion

In my view, not much has changed about the core of the story other than the recent deal. Cintas Corporation remains a high-quality compounder with durable growth, strong margins, and a proven ability to execute both organically and inorganically. As illustrated, the UniFirst deal will likely add a compelling long-term layer to that thesis, and I think management has earned it given their track record. That said, I’m not convinced the current valuation gives investors enough margin for error in the near term and so even after the pullback the stock still trades at an above market multiple that assumes a lot goes right. I’d prefer a wider margin of safety before stepping in. For now, I’m staying patient and maintaining a ‘hold’ rating. This is a name I want to own, just not at this price.

data.title: The Final Push Toward A Deal And Why I'm Now Fully Invested
data.description:
Happy Friday, everyone!

I hope it's a green day in your portfolio as well. As we've seen in the past few days, markets do not wait for certainty, and the past few days in the Middle East have made that painfully clear (for the bears, of course).

It is easy to get headline fatigue (I am almost burned out, to be honest), but I think we are in the last sprint before we can take a break from checking major media outlets every now and then.

Markets have quickly shifted the tone from fear to optimism after the U.S.-brokered ceasefire between Israel and Lebanon, Iran’s decision to keep the Strait of Hormuz open to commercial traffic (with caveats), and reports of progress toward a broader U.S.-Iran nuclear understanding.

As I'm writing, I'm seeing pretty much every sector in the green, except energy and utilities.

In this piece, I cover what to watch for in what I believe is the final sprint of the Middle East conflict. I will also provide a rundown of how I shifted from neutral to bullish as soon as April 9.

On the risk side, I admit there is a lot of optimism grounded in one single pillar: a deal that puts an end to the war and reopens the Strait of Hormuz. If the second round of talks fails, I believe the rally we've seen over the last few days will quickly revert.

My view is that there is light at the end of the tunnel. Given the recent development, I depleted my 19% cash position, and I'm heading into the weekend talks fully invested.

A Quick Recap Of The Developments in The Middle East

There are three main headlines that caught my eye these last few days.

The first one is the ceasefire between Israel and Lebanon, brokered by the U.S. and agreed on April 16.

There are three main points that I wanted to highlight from the ceasefire, as they are quite significant for Lebanon.

As for Israel, it seems that its military remains as much as 10 km inside southern Lebanon as part of a buffer zone to prevent further attacks. In other words, this seems like another fragile ceasefire, largely propped up by the U.S., as noted by Trump's post:

Israel will not be bombing Lebanon any longer. They are PROHIBITED from doing so by the U.S.A. Enough is enough!!!

The second headline (related to the one above) that caught the market's attention was the announcement by Iran’s foreign minister on 17 April that the Strait of Hormuz is open for all commercial vessels for the remaining period of the 10-day Lebanon ceasefire.

Interestingly, President Trump went further and said in a post that Iran had agreed never to close Hormuz again.

Now, the details matter. According to a Reuters report, a senior Iranian official said that all ships can sail through, including U.S. commercial vessels, but passage must be coordinated with the IRGC and Iran’s Ports and Maritime Organization.

Notably, transit appears to remain limited to lanes Iran deems safe, and military vessels remain prohibited from transiting.

How about the U.S. blockade? Well, it appears that the U.S. naval blockade remains active for traffic to and from Iranian ports until the U.S.-Iran deal is complete.

This brings me to the third headline:

According to Bloomberg, Trump said in a phone interview that Iran agreed to an indefinite suspension of its nuclear program and would receive no frozen U.S. funds. On top of that, he noted that talks over a lasting deal will "probably" be held over the weekend, noting "most of the main points are finalized. It’ll go pretty quickly."

As I'm writing this piece, Iran hasn't confirmed this concession.

However, the markets have spoken:

As I'm writing, I have a 0.4% cash position in my portfolio. A few days back, I had a 19% cash position that I decided to keep on the sidelines during the weekend (just in case the talks fail for the second time). Not anymore. Below, I explain why (and how) I turned bullish in the broader markets.

Why (And How) I Turned Bullish

At first, I wasn't sold on the idea of a ceasefire.

Back on March 20, I published a piece titled: "Even With Hormuz Reopened, I Still See The S&P 500 At 6,100."

Here is the rationale from that article:

The catalyst?

Inflation is likely to remain high between March and June (at least), as the oil flows will take a few months to normalize after the conflict eventually comes to an end in the Middle East. Therefore, the post-war relief rally shouldn't last more than a few weeks.

Back then, I was overly concerned that price hikes would spill over beyond transportation services or airfares. I mean, things you buy in a supermarket don't just happen to appear on the shelves, right?

Therefore, if fuel (particularly diesel) prices spiked, why would corporations decide not to pass through the additional fuel costs?

Add on top of fuel inflation, shortages in fertilizer (urea, ammonia, and broader nitrogen), both due to the Hormuz blockade and Ukrainian attacks on Russian plants, and the outlook doesn't seem to be particularly exciting for risk assets.

Back in late March, the icing on the cake was the skepticism I noted from both "expert" economists and prediction markets about rate cuts. Even today, the CME Group survey data is showing the most likely date for a rate cut as June 2027.

Therefore, I didn't fully buy the March core CPI forecast from Cleveland's Fed inflation nowcasting tool. For reference, here is another excerpt from my late March 2026 article that shows the precise forecast back then:

The latest update from the Cleveland Fed nowcast shows headline CPI at 3.02% y/y, core CPI at 2.60% y/y, headline PCE at 3.14% y/y, and core PCE at 2.97% y/y for March:

I thought that was too conservative, as the core CPI for March (which excludes energy and food prices) was projected at only 2.60%. For reference, the core CPI in February was 2.5%.

What happens with apparel, recreation, and household furnishings prices (I was wondering in my mind)? In hindsight, my skepticism of the inflation nowcast turned out to be a mistake.

Fast forward a few weeks, and the BEA released the CPI report for March. It turns out that core CPI came in at exactly 2.6%. That's why I wrote a piece on April 10, titled: "March CPI Just Flipped The Inflation Debate."

Why?

With inflation fears mostly out of the way, I believe the market is now underestimating the number of rate cuts this year (remember the table above from the CME Group). As I discussed multiple times, I think Kevin Warsh will cut interest rates this year, despite the current skepticism. Here is the rationale from my April 10 article:

Therefore, if the April/May CPI shows a similar result, likely incoming Fed Chair Kevin Warsh will have one more reason to (aggressively) cut interest rates, on top of the one below:

That said, my first strong buy rating after the start of the conflict in the Middle East came in one day earlier, on April 9, from an article titled: "This Ceasefire Rally Could Collapse Tomorrow - Here's What I'm Buying Anyway."

If you read that piece, you may notice how skeptical I was about the fragile ceasefire. However, I decided to look beyond the near-term pressures and deploy part of the capital that I've been raising since late January this year.

I'm glad I did, because a few days later came the news that the U.S. had blockaded vessels bound for Iran, and that was when I connected the dots in my article, "Why the U.S. Blockade May Be the Move That Ends the War."

Looking Ahead

We're not out of the woods yet.

As I'm writing, Iran hasn't confirmed the nuclear concessions, and the transits through the Strait of Hormuz are still in coordination with the IRGC using lanes that Iran deems as safe. Therefore, I cannot help but wonder whether the toll booth between Larak Island and Qeshm Island is still in effect.

As for normal passage through the strait, there is still a lot of uncertainty due to possible mines along the route that vessels used before the conflict began. If mines were actually laid, clearing that area will take some time.

In regards to the number of transits, there were 8 ships on the first full day of the U.S. blockade, including three Iran-linked tankers. In other words, the traffic was barely affected.

The big question over the weekend, or until the April 22 deadline, is whether there will be a final deal between the U.S. and Iran. Given the recent developments, I think there's a good chance the answer is yes. At the very least, I expect the extension of a ceasefire and some concessions from both sides regarding Hormuz transits (including the U.S. blockade of Iran-bound vessels).

The risk is the fragility of the talks. If the second round of talks ends without a deal, and the conflict resumes on April 22, I believe the rally will quickly revert. I am willing to take this risk, but I'm mindful that the chance of this draconian scenario is not zero.

data.title: CGCV: Likely To Underperform IVV Owing Low Beta Names That Restrain Upside Capture
data.description:
I believe that the Capital Group Conservative Equity ETF (CGCV) will underperform the iShares Core S&P 500 ETF (IVV) this year, as the low-beta ingredient that bolstered its returns in January–February will likely become a detractor. To be clear, I expect the S&P 500 to reach 7,550 by the end of the year, which implies an around 6% gain from its level as of writing this article. It is unlikely that CGCV will keep pace with it.

Given the Strait of Hormuz situation that was the main contributor to expectations for higher inflation and potentially even higher interest rates is resolved, I anticipate equities sporting strong growth (and especially those having robust GARP metrics) characteristics to outperform those in the value and low volatility camps. CGCV is underweight in growth, has unappealing GARP metrics, and is overweight in low volatility and value, which will restrain its upside capture, and there is historical data supporting that point. I do not see a long-term alpha potential either, again owing to its tilt towards less volatile names that are inherently incapable of capturing sufficient market upside during bull markets.

With that being said, as I have already explained in the August 2025 article, CGCV has a sensible strategy and it does deliver on its three objectives of "current income, growth of capital and conservation of principal," which secures a Hold rating. Also, I still believe that the Capital Group Dividend Value ETF (CGDV) is a stronger option both factor- and performance-wise, and I will explain why below in the note.

CGCV Strategy And Portfolio

As we know from the Q4 fact sheet available on the Capital Group website, the actively managed CGCV

...strives for the balanced accomplishment of three objectives: current income, growth of capital and conservation of principal.

It is also explained that CGCV is "conservatively managed to reduce volatility and risk," so it "...has the potential to provide downside resilience during market declines." And as a mild spoiler, I should mention that it does deliver on these fronts but with nuances.

CGCV Portfolio Discussion

I believe that at this juncture, the CGCV portfolio is offering mostly robust factor exposures, mainly in terms of value and quality (there is room for improvement though), which could secure a downside capture ratio of around 80% if the market narrative once again switches towards a more pessimistic one, for instance, if investors start to price in an new round of escalation or question the AI growth premia, etc.

However, my concern here is that a sufficient allocation to growth (and especially GARP) names is clearly lacking, which, together with its tilt towards less volatile names, will likely restrain its upside capture this year and quite likely beyond, translating into underperformance versus IVV.

More specifically, as of April 10, there were 71 equities in the CGCV portfolio accounting for 96.6% of the net assets. Another asset class represented was cash & cash equivalents. Microsoft Corporation (MSFT) was the key holding with an around 4.99% weight, followed by Broadcom Inc. (AVGO) that accounted for 4.22%. Importantly, my analysis shows that nearly all CGCV's holdings are S&P 500 constituents (90.5% of the net assets). There are only five exceptions, key among them being Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited (TSM).

Now, let us delve deeper into the factor story.

Value and Growth

While CGCV does not ignore the trillion-dollar league completely, it is much lighter in these bellwethers compared to both CGDV and IVV (which tracks its primary benchmark), which is reflected in its weighted average market cap of around $640 billion.

In part as a consequence of that, we see much more robust value characteristics even compared to CGDV (which is, as a reminder, a "dividend value ETF"), including a higher adjusted weighted average earnings yield, a lower Price/Sales ratio (no exposure to NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA) helped a lot), etc.

But as I have multiple times explained in my previous articles discussing ETFs, large exposure to the value factor requires compromises, chiefly in terms of growth. And this is exactly the case with CGCV, as its growth metrics are considerably weaker compared to the peers I selected, especially its WA forward EPS growth rate. As a consequence, it is also much lighter in GARP names, as illustrated by the Price/Earnings-to-Growth, the Price/Sales-to-Growth, and the EV/EBITDA-to-Growth ratios.

Quality

I would not say that something is fundamentally wrong with CGCV's quality characteristics, as they are generally fine, but I still believe that it is somewhat underdelivering on that front. For a portfolio that is dominated by mega-caps and has no footprint in mid-caps, an 83.5% allocation to stocks with a B- Quant Profitability grade or higher is too small, especially compared to IVV and CGDV. Its weighted average capital efficiency metrics like the adjusted ROE, ROA, and ROTC also look much weaker.

Low volatility

CGCV maintains a considerable footprint in the information technology sector, precisely like IVV and CGDV. But what makes it considerably different is a much larger allocation to defensive sectors like consumer staples and utilities (18.4% combined weight compared to IVV's 7.4% and 7.37% of CGDV).

In part as a consequence of that (nearly all its holdings from these sectors have 24-month beta coefficients below 0.6, with the only exception being Constellation Energy Corporation (CEG)), the WA beta coefficients of its portfolio are significantly below those of IVV and CGDV, as per my calculations.

Metrics CGCV CGDV IVV
24M Beta 0.78 1.07 1.12
60M Beta 0.84 1.07 1.14

Calculated by the author using data from Seeking Alpha and the ETFs

And while this fact have both positive (i.e., it secures a less deep maximum drawdown in a correction) and negative effects, I am gravitating toward the view that it will almost certainly restrain CGCV's ability to mirror IVV's gains this year. For clarity, I still anticipate the S&P 500 to touch 7,550 by the end of the year.

CGCV Performance

CGCV offers mostly solid historical performance, as it has gained more than 28% since its inception in June 2024. However, as usual, it is all about nuances.

For example, the low volatility and value factors most likely secured its outperformance versus IVV in January–February (0.5% and 3.42% stronger total returns, respectively). However, I believe that these exact factors were likely the culprits for its underperformance in 2024 and 2025. So since its inception, even though it had a moment earlier this year, CGCV has underperformed the S&P 500 ETF, let alone CGDV.

At the same time, CGCV, despite its conservative strategy, underperformed IVV by 1.14% in March, as it was surprisingly less prepared to tackle the ripple effects from the Iran war.

Next, looking at the data for July 2024–March 2026, we see that while CGCV outperformed IVV during the period, delivering a 0.41% active return, it had an upside capture of only 90.77% compared to CGDV's 121.19%.

So the corollary here is that unless the bearish mood settles over the market again, which I do not anticipate, CGCV will not be able to keep pace with IVV this year and more likely beyond.

Final Thoughts

To conclude, I anticipate the Capital Group Conservative Equity ETF to underperform IVV this year (i.e., deliver less than 6% gain from the current levels), which is the main rationale behind the Hold rating.

Nevertheless, CGCV still could be an interesting option for investors seeking to reduce volatility a little without sacrificing too much of their exposure to the trillion-dollar bellwethers and remain mostly within the S&P 500 universe. CGCV also scores nicely against the liquidity metrics, and its expense ratio is adequate at 33 bps.

However, it is worth keeping in mind that its considerable allocation to low beta defensive names, together with small exposure to growth, can restrain its upside capture, resulting in underperformance versus IVV over the medium- to longer-term.

In this regard, I believe investors seeking higher growth rates and stronger quality and the potential for outperformance this year and more likely beyond should take a closer look at CGDV instead, especially considering its robust performance. All in all, I maintain the Hold rating on CGCV.

data.title: SCHB: SCHB: A Hold Amid Premium Valuations And Higher Risk-Free Rates
data.description:
Schwab US Broad Market ETF (SCHB) primarily tracks the Dow Jones U.S. Broad Stock Market Index. As a result, it very closely resembles the S&P 500 and its index tracking fund (SPY), though with broader market exposure. The returns over the last ten years have been almost identical, with SCHB slightly underperforming SPY.

SCHB predominantly remains a proxy for broad-based allocation to U.S. equities. With an expense ratio of 0.03% and passive index tracking, this is a low-cost index fund for passive investors who simply seek exposure to U.S. equities. However, at present, due to elevated equity valuations, higher earnings multiples, lower yields, and higher risk-free rates, the proposition for allocating assets to broad-based equities, whether via SCHB or SPY, warrants cautious evaluation.

Portfolio Allocation

The current allocation is tilted towards the technology sector, followed by financials, with just a little under ~50% combined exposure to these two sectors. The current sector allocation is similar to that of the S&P 500. However, with reference to holdings, Schwab U.S. Broad Market ETF (SCHB) holds around ~2,400 securities at present, with some exposure to mid to small cap subset as well, although the primary constitution remains predominantly large-cap.

The top ten holdings are largely technology-focused businesses. Leaving Berkshire Hathaway Inc. (BRK.B) aside, virtually every other holding is highly impacted and sensitive to the technological developments and thereby reflects a little more uncertainty for formulating future expectations. The dividend yield is modest, and the returns are attributable largely to the long-term capital appreciation of the underlying securities.

Although there might be nothing inherently wrong with the current allocation, considering SCHB is a broad-based index fund, nevertheless, especially for value-focused investors, when we also take into account current geopolitical developments along with a possibility of valuation compression in mega-cap tech; this composition might not be via itself possessing a sufficient margin of safety.

Forward Expectations

The current price to earnings for the index is at ~27x. This is not suggestive of attractive forward return expectations for the time being. This remains the case even with recent volatility and increased macro-uncertainty in the present period, which should rather have warranted some caution at current valuations.

The above chart reflects the historical Price to Earnings multiple of the S&P 500 index, but since the holding allocation and the ratios are very much identical for both indices, a reasonable inference about valuations could be developed using the S&P index multiples. The current P/E of ~27x is slightly above the ten-year averages. The ROE of ~25%, is priced in by the current P/B of ~4.65x along with earnings multiples which already reflect the premiums paid by the investors for allocating to broader US market companies. With the major allocation to mega-cap tech companies, which also carry higher return on capital, the ROE metrics at the same time become enhanced as well.

The recent five-year EPS growth rate has been decent, however, extrapolating that would not be entirely correct. In addition, the CAPE (cyclically adjusted P/E ratio) remains elevated as well. The current value stands at ~36x. Kindly note that this data point differs according to the methodology and across platforms, however, the broader point of multiples remaining elevated is illustrated well.

Moving further on near-term developments, the recent Job Reports, suggested that the labour markets relatively healthy. The Job growth beat expectations by a wide margin, and the unemployment rate, may also give the Fed some more room to maintain their focus on inflation control. However, at the same time, it also reduces the expectations of near-term rate cuts. Eventually, risk-free interest rates impact the denominator, which is the required rate of return for both bonds and stocks. While the impact in the former is more visible instantly, eventually common stocks as an asset class would also be impacted by the same. The 10- year Treasury yield rose to ~4.35% after the report was released. Considering these mixed factors, the point which we can reflect on is that while the confidence markets might be valid in certain contexts, yet the current market valuations do not leave much room or margin of safety, should something unexpected come by. The valuation multiples remain stretched and the value is attributable more to the growth outlook of mega-cap companies rather than current earnings power.

Buffet Indicator Suggestive of Expensive Valuations

The market valuation proxy, famously provided by Warren Buffett, which is the Market Capitalization to GDP Ratio, suggests caution as well. Kindly note the above Buffett Indicator is more of a measure of long-term return expectation rather than near- to medium-term. In addition, this is a rough proxy and is not something which should always be looked at in isolation. Still, however, this single measure neatly reflects how market expectations stand at any given moment. The current stock market levels seem to already incorporate very high growth expectations and reflect premium valuations for the broader market. The Buffett Indicator, thereby, is also suggestive of the markets being meaningfully elevated at present. While this might in itself might not be reflective of a chance of negative returns or market corrections, what it clearly indicates is the long-term rate of return for investors to be meaningfully lowered due to an expensive entry point.

Treasuries vs Equities and A Case for Tactical Liquidity

I would be rating this as a hold because for many investors, who do not look for tactical exposures or market mispricing, but rather just want to be allocated to equities via a low-cost, broad-based simple ETF, index tracking funds like SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust SPY or Schwab U.S. Large-Cap Value ETF SCHV remain of use. Leaving that aspect, however, I might have also considered this to be a sell at present, for switching to something better. To reflect more on the latter aspect, we can also compare the current prospects of SCHB with other asset classes for forming future return expectations

At present, US Treasuries yield ~4.25%. The trailing earnings yield for Schwab U.S. Broad Market ETF (SCHB), which is the inverse of P/E, happens to be ~3.7%. Of course, this alone is not sufficient in isolation for relative comparison, but I have already highlighted the levels of stretched valuations for SCHB. In that case, even holding cash for an enterprising investor also becomes a reasonable proposition. By doing that, an investor allows themselves to be in a position to capitalise on market shocks and downward corrections. At present, however, an investor, while sitting in cash, would also be getting yields comparable to riskier equity earnings yields. This is simple to understand, yet has a lot of importance when deciding on capital allocations. If and when opportunity arises, an investor with dry powder can capitalise on them versus one who does not possess any and is completely allocated and not much room to buy on sharp declines caused due to other than fundamental reasons. Carrying this further, some long-dated AAA rated municipal bonds were recently trading at a yield of ~4%, which effectively represents a tax-equivalent yield of around ~6–6.5%. Propositions like this make much more sense than they are mentioned about, especially in time of premium market multiples and valuations.

SCHB On Hold at Present

Taking into account all of this cumulatively, the current general stock market trajectory presents a delicate situation to commit capital. While it still remains a good vehicle for investors who might have already allocated to these index funds as a part of their allocations and are not much concerned about medium-term volatility, the current entry point, at least in my view, does not incorporate necessary margin of safety, and there might be one or the other more favorable investment avenues that might be looked upon by investors before allocating money to SCHB. The one such avenue was allocation to cash in form of treasury. An investor could also consider corporate debts or municipality bonds as per their considerations and preferences. Therefore, I would rate SCHB as a hold for now.

Limitations to the analysis

The current inherent uncertainty in the broader stock markets poses risks to investors in either direction. Any severe escalation of current geopolitical events, along with trade-related disruptions and underlying unpredictability on part of the US administration, incorporates a chance of sharp market corrections. Investors holding this ETF, therefore, are exposed to the risk of not being able to book the profits which they would have been able to generate due to strong recent performances. At the same time, this price volatility could be on the upside as well, and there is also a possibility wherein stock market indices, due to deliberate injection of liquidity, future rate cuts, etc., might rally significantly over short-term, similar to how it happened in 2020, even if the external environment remains sluggish. Further, the gap between GDP and Market Capitalization could continue to widen, with financial markets trading more on liquidity and speculative flows rather than true fundamental valuation.

All this poses a delicate problem and a hindsight bias for investors, wherein they might not be able to correctly identify the reasons driving return expectations for the markets. The current situation, with heightened uncertainty and frequent developments, further increases the unpredictability of any analysis.

In Conclusion

SCHB, for the time being, is kept on hold, with it not being able to offer any compelling investment proposition for a fresh buy at current levels. This is valid on both a standalone as well as a relative basis. The valuation indicators remain suggestive of premium valuations which direct tend to impact the long-range return expectations. An investor might also want to compare the current stock market yields with treasury and risk-free rates carefully for evaluating if the incremental prospects offered by equities at the time being are compelling enough for an allocation.

data.title: Q1 Brings Some Improvements To Regions Financial
data.description:
Regional banking giant Regions Financial Corporation (RF) reported the results from Q1 2026 before the market opened today. The company stated diluted adjusted earnings of $0.62 per share, which topped expectations by $0.03, or 5.1%. Total revenue of $1.87 billion fell just short of previous projections, missing by $50 million. The market has shown mixed reactions, with the price of RF falling prior to the opening bell and then climbing modestly on an overall strong day for the major indices.

Regions Financial has been a company that I have followed for many years. The company continues to benefit from its geographic footprint, which includes fast-growing markets in the Southeast. A low cost of deposits has helped boost its net interest margin and provided greater runway for net interest income. However, the last several months have not been kind to RF, as it has only added 3.6% in price since January 1, even though its 12-month gains exceed 45%.

I last wrote about Regions after its Q4 2025 earnings were released on January 16. I downgraded its stock to a Hold at that time, pointing to a few concerns that appeared in the company’s final financial update from last year. Larger banks are rarely able to make significant improvements in their operations in a short period of time, but I do see some progress being made by the company in Q1. I still consider RF to be a Hold, but it is closer to the Buy category than it was at the start of the year.

Company Snapshot

Regions Financial serves as the holding company for Regions Bank, which is listed as the 26th largest U.S. bank by the Federal Reserve based on total assets. The company is a member of the S&P 500 Index and offers banking, wealth management, and mortgage services to customers in 15 states. The largest shares of its roughly 1,250 branch locations can be found in Florida (277), Alabama (245), Tennessee (242), Georgia (128), and Mississippi (127). Readers who want more details about RF and its operations may want to look back at the first article I wrote about the company in early February of 2025.

Total assets for the company crossed over the $160 billion mark in Q1 2026, finishing at $160.7 billion, 1.2% more than at the end of 2025. The bulk of those assets are connected to the $97.9 billion in gross loans held by the bank at the end of the quarter. Even as its loan portfolio has grown by 2.3% over the last 12 months, its allowance for loan losses has declined by 5.3%, reflecting the bank’s assessment of improved credit quality and decreases in specific reserve borrowers connected to loan charge-offs.

Business loans comprise just over two-thirds of the bank’s loan portfolio, driven by a sizeable allocation to commercial and industrial loans. This category of loans was valued at $50.8 billion, or 51.9% of the total as of March 31. Commercial real estate mortgages, when investor CRE and owner-occupied CRE are combined, make up just about 13% of the bank's loan mix. The largest share of RF's consumer loans belongs to home mortgages at $19.6 billion, or 20.1%. The segment of Region’s loans that are connected to residential mortgages has declined almost 1% over the last 12 months as the real estate market, even in higher growth areas like the Southeast, has been particularly challenging due to mortgage rate and home supply issues.

No discussion of Regions Bank is complete without the consideration of its deposits, which have some of the lowest costs in the industry. At the end of Q1, RF reported $131.9 billion in deposit accounts, a 0.6% increase from the end of last year. About 30.4% of those deposits are in non-interest-bearing accounts, an increase of 0.4% from the end of 2025. The total cost of deposits for Regions Bank at the end of Q1 2026 was 1.20%. The most recent data available from the FDIC puts the average cost of deposits for a bank of RF’s size around 2.12%, showing the competitive advantage that the company holds over its peers.

Regions Financial pays a quarterly dividend of $0.265 per share. The company has raised its annual distribution for 13 consecutive years. The current payout gives RF a forward yield of 3.80%, 89 basis points better than the sector median. Over the last decade, Regions’ compound average dividend growth rate is 15.9%.

Analysis Of Regions Financial

The $0.62 adjusted EPS reported in Q1 2026 was $0.05 better than Q4 2025 and $0.08, or 14.8%, higher than Q1 2025. Adjusted net income of $539 million was $35 million ahead of the previous quarter and $52 million, or 10.7%, better than the same period from last year. Most of the bank’s key performance metrics have improved in the last three months of reported operations, as shown in the table below, with only net interest margin showing a small decline. All measurements are better than the corresponding quarter from last year.

Q1 2026

Q4 2025

Q1 2025

Return on Assets

1.42%

1.34%

1.27%

Return on Equity

12.35%

11.58%

11.49%

Net Interest Margin

3.67%

3.70%

3.52%

Efficiency Ratio (Adj.)

56.6%

57.5%

56.8%

Areas of the company’s operations that are not directly tied to interest rates slipped slightly over the last three months as Regions Financial experienced a decline in both wealth management income and card fees. Wealth management income for Q1 was $141 million, down 1.4% from Q4 2025, but still 9.3% better than Q1 2025. Card and ATM fee income fell almost 5% in the last three months to $117 million. Income from capital markets was up 3.8% from Q4 of last year to $83 million, and the company stated that this category could rise to a range of $90 million to $105 million later this year.

Asset quality for Regions Bank did show improvement in Q1 as the company’s ratios for net charge-offs and nonperforming loans declined from the last three months of 2025, as shown in the image below. The company’s business services criticized the loans ratio of 5.15%, which was an improvement of 16 basis points from Q4 of last year. RF expects FY 2026 net charge-offs to be in the range of 0.40% to 0.50%, which is slightly below average based on data available from all banks from last year.

Other expectations for the remainder of 2026 include modest increases of 2.5% to 4% for net interest income, a 3% to 5% improvement in non-interest income, and controlled increases in non-interest expenses of 1.5% to 3.5%. Loan and deposit growth are projected to rise in the low single digits. Net interest margin is expected to stay in a relatively tight range from where it is now to just better than where it ended last year.

Valuation Discussion

RF reported $2.30 in diluted earnings per share in FY 2025, which was 19.2% better than what was earned in FY 2024. In FY 2026, analysts expect the company to post EPS of $2.63, a 14.3% expansion from last year. This gives the stock a forward P/E of 10.62 based on FY 2026 estimates. Seeking Alpha’s quant rating assigns a “B-” to the stock’s valuation, with both trailing and forward earnings multiples hovering right around average for its sector.

The company should get a boost from its continued share repurchases, which have been ongoing for several years. On December 10, 2025, the company announced a $3 billion share repurchase authorization, a value that equaled over 12% of Regions’ total current market cap at that time. In Q1 2026, Regions Financial bought back roughly 14 million shares of its common stock in the open market at a total cost of $401 million.

Tangible book value per share stood at $13.69 at the end of Q1. That was an increase of 11% from the end of Q1 2025. At its current price, the stock trades at a price-to-book value ratio of 2.06. When I last wrote about the company, this ratio was just 2.01. Anything here over 2.0 tends to lead me to believe that the stock’s current price has a lot of optimism already baked in, leaving less room for additional price appreciation in the short term.

Risks To Consider

The risks to consider before making any new investments in Regions Financial are similar to those that I have discussed in past articles. The company is dependent on healthy economic growth in the states in which it operates, especially those in the Southeast. The company has over half of its branch locations in three states—Florida, Alabama, and Tennessee. All appears stable for now, with Florida still one of the fastest-growing states in the country. Changes in these trends, especially in the states where RF has the greatest footprint, could make loan growth more challenging and pressure asset quality for existing loans.

The company has stated a goal of penetrating deeper into its existing growing markets with a plan to build 135 to 150 new branches over the next five years. Regions may also close as many branches in areas where population shifts or wealth migrations are making some communities less profitable. The capital investments required to build new branches and renovate old ones will add to the company’s operating costs during a time when some banks are moving toward what is known as a “branch light” model as more financial transactions are done online. In Q1 2025, 78% of RF’s customer transactions were digital. In Q1 2026, that number had grown to 80%. Mobile banking logins for the company grew 8.8% in the last 12 months. Zelle transactions increased 23.0%. There are no guarantees that Region’s plans for physical growth will produce the expected profit growth going forward.

Final Thoughts

When I first wrote about Regions Financial 14 months ago, I called its stock a Buy. When I downgraded it in January of this year, it was not because of any major concerns, but rather a belief that the price appreciation it experienced earlier in 2025 had caused its valuation to become more in line with its peers. RF still has the lowest P/E ratios of any of its peers listed on Seeking Alpha. U.S. Bancorp (USB) and PNC Financial Services (PNC) are the closest at 10.95 and 11.76, respectively. However, Regions also has the highest PEG ratio, showing that the lower current P/E valuation may be justified. I still believe its stock will perform largely in step with most others from its sector, and I am maintaining my Hold rating for now.

data.title: Market Close: The Most Important Price Of The Day
data.description:
By Phil Mackintosh, Nasdaq Chief Economist

The official close is undeniably the most important trade of the day.

It determines stock and index returns. It is used for futures and options settlements, mutual fund cash flows and exchange-traded funds (ETF) creations and redemptions.

It is also used to determine the volatility of stocks, the tracking error of index funds and the outperformance (or alpha) of portfolio managers.

In short, it is important.

Close is the largest trade every day

Because the close price is critically important to so many traders, the close is also usually the largest single trade of the day.

As the data below shows, the close usually accounts for more than 10% of the whole day's trading. In some countries, like Japan and developed countries in Europe, it is much more.

Chart 1: The official close is the largest trade of the day

Because of that, investors and issuers care about the close (a lot!). They prefer it to be predictable and stable - reducing, rather than adding, to trading costs. That requires a “good” close to be:

  • Low volatility
  • Low market impact
  • High liquidity
  • Not subject to last-minute shocks or orders
  • Be a price many investors can participate at

Given the close is such a large trade - and such an important trade - the market structure around closes is especially important. It needs to:

  • Attract both buyers and sellers
  • Respond to supply and demand
  • Match numerous different buyers and sellers
  • Produce efficient prices (unbiased estimators)
  • Be simple and understandable to participants

One challenge is balancing the transparency required to attract offsetting liquidity for these very large trades, while limiting the information leakage that might make large investor trades cost more.

As index funds grow, the close is becoming even more important

One important participant in the close are index funds, which are required to run very low tracking error. A liquid and reliable close makes the indexes investable and replicable. Data shows that index funds are growing, which no doubt has been contributing to the growing size of closes worldwide.

However, as we noted here, indexers usually represent just a fraction of close trading. The close is also made up of mutual fund cash flows, options hedging and other trades, which take advantage of a relatively low cost of liquidity (close is usually when spreads and volatility have fallen the most). There are also certain closes around derivative expirations and index rebalances, where the close is even more important, resulting in an even greater percentage of average daily volume.

Chart 2: The official close is the largest trade of the day

There are four main ways to close the market

In trying to optimize the close market structure, there is almost constant evolution and improvement of close processes. But in general, there are four main ways to “close” the stock market. In order of sophistication and complexity:

1. Last Trade

This works just like it sounds. When the market stops trading for the day, whatever the last trade was priced at becomes the “official close.” It's simple.

But this can mean that the close is set by a very small trade, which also makes it almost impossible for indexers to match the close.

2. Weighted-average price (WAP)

An extension on using just the last trade is to use a collection of trades at the end of the day where the price is weighted by the size or volume of each trade, resulting in a weighted-average price (WAP) that better reflects overall market activity during the closing period.

That means the close is set by a larger proportion of liquidity and reduces any “rush of activity” as everyone tries to be the last trade, but it’s still almost impossible for indexers to match the close.

This is mostly used by smaller, less liquid markets - without large index trackers.

3. Auction

Almost all markets start with an auction that computes the equilibrium price where supply and demand clears. Many markets, especially in Europe, also use a discrete (separate) auction to discover close price, too.

This has the benefit of being easy for indexers to match the close - even on very large volumes. However, there can sometimes be dislocations (or price moves) from continuous prices when the auction is unexpectedly imbalanced (has many more buyers or sellers), which can happen on large index trade days.

4. On Close

In North America, a different version of the close auction has evolved. Rather than starting to build the book for an auction after trading stops for the day, it accepts orders (and publishes imbalances) while continuous trading is happening.

That allows market makers to hedge for the auction with continuous volumes. It also tends to make the dislocations (or price moves) from continuous prices to the auction much smaller. However, by definition, more information about the close imbalance is leaked to other traders while tickers are still trading.

Chart 3: Four main ways to “close” the market

Most modern markets use auctions

If we look at closing mechanisms over time, we see that just 30 years ago it was most common to use the last traded price!

A lot has changed in 30 years. Most markets went from open outcry to electronic during the 1990s, the U.S. markets adopted decimal prices, index funds and program trading was created, and volumes exploded.

So, it’s really not surprising that the way markets close has also changed.

In the chart below, we start with data from an academic study. It shows that by 2014, most markets were already using standalone auctions to aggregate large volumes of trades, match them all together and set official closing prices.

We updated this data for a sample of more recent years using a slightly different approach (grey box in the chart below for 2018 onwards, where we also separate WAP and last trades).

We see that the trends of modernization continue. However, with a broader universe, we also see that some countries use weighted-average price auctions. We would highlight that these are used in smaller and less-liquid markets and are also in decline.

Chart 4: Markets have evolved from “last trade” to mostly using auctions for close

Why don’t North American markets rely on auctions alone at the close

One important difference between European and North American markets can be seen in the chart below.

  • When Asian and European markets close, there is other liquidity available in other countries.
  • At the end of North American trading, there is a period where no markets are open, making liquidity harder to source and price.

This makes it much easier to hedge unexpected imbalances that arise in the auctions in Europe and Asia than in the U.S. market.

However, we should also highlight that U.S. stocks and futures markets do still trade after the official close at 4 p.m. Eastern Time.

Chart 5: European markets mostly use auctions; North America uses on-close

The overlap with continuous trading and the close auction book-build is designed to be able to access some of the end-of-day liquidity to hedge the close.

In studies we’ve done, we see that the market reacts immediately to imbalance messages, which the close auction book-build shares. Prices rise (for buy imbalances) and fall (for sell imbalances) - in proportion to the size of the imbalance (line thickness).

Chart 6: MOC volatility in the U.S. is generally pretty low thanks to trading into the close

Some consider this information leakage detrimental to those trying to execute at the close, as it gives other traders a chance to profit from the trades in the close book.

However, as the chart above shows, even for large imbalances, the overall impact or “leakage” from the imbalance announcement is less than 15 basis points. In addition, the difference between the last trade price (red or blue line) and the actual close (parallel grey lines of the same thickness) is, on average, a fraction of that.

In short, the on-close facilities bring additional liquidity into the close - at a pretty cheap price (for liquidity) and a small average profit.

Other ways to reduce volatility

Issuers and investors typically lose when large trades cause prices to move dramatically into the close. There are a couple of interesting ways different markets try to offset that use:

  1. Order types that only offset imbalances - From market orders that only trade on the “opposite” side of the auction book imbalance to limit orders that offset adverse price moves caused by imbalance selling (limit buys) or imbalance buying (limit sells).
  2. Extension period - To focus more attention and liquidity on the stocks that did not originally have an “orderly” close.
  3. Random closes - Make it harder for traders to add or cancel large trades at the very last minute, giving investors and arbitrageurs time to offset excessive market impact.

Chart 7: Some closes use collars to stop prices moving “too far” in the close

Another important feature of the close is the way it aggregates all orders into one book, which helps to minimize impact and ensure all investors get the same price.

If closes were fragmented like live trading often is, there would be multiple clearing prices, depending on which venue orders rested in. This is a problem for investors, as they are the marginal demand and supply.

That means that the market with more buyers clears at a higher price, while the market with more sellers will clear at a lower price. That leaves investors, as a whole, receiving worse prices than they would in a consolidated book.

In continuous markets, these costs are small, as arbitrage can occur between venues.

Auction markets happen instantly. That leaves no time to hedge or send new orders. As a result, market makers run execution and position risks - and liquidity costs would rise.

Chart 8: Multiple closes would most likely hurt investors

As markets change, closes continue to evolve

The trends we see in Chart 4 are far from over. Even markets that have long adopted a specific kind of close regularly reoptimize the rules. For example:

  • In 2016, the Hong Kong exchange moved from a time weighted-average Price (WAP) style close to an auction, in conjunction with a new volatility control mechanism.
  • In 2019, Nasdaq delayed the cutoff to enter orders but also introduced late LOCs (limit orders that could be entered even later) as a way to reduce leakage and still offset unexpected price moves.
  • In 2021, Toronto changed how the Canadian market close works, making it more similar to U.S. models

Close has always been important - to investors, academics and traders.

It's earned a market structure all of its own.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: PagSeguro: Fairly Valued At 9x P/E (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
This is my second coverage of PagSeguro Digital Ltd. (PAGS). I first wrote about the company in February 2025 and issued a Strong Buy rating. Since my article, the company has outperformed the market with a 35% return. At that time, the stock was an underappreciated asset that was in a severe downtrend due to a weak Brazilian real (BRL:USD). I believe that now the stock is fairly valued. Perhaps there is still some money left on the table, but I doubt that the stock can beat the market returns from now on. Therefore, I am rating the stock a Hold, as I believe there are better picks in the stock market right now.

In this update, I will talk about the macro situation, their latest quarter, and lastly provide my opinion on the current valuation.

Recent Events and the Macro Environment

In the previous article, I noted that the Selic (Brazilian interest rate) was around 10.5% and used that in my calculation. In the end, interest rates went much higher, and as the company stated, the Selic "grew from an average of around 10.8% per year in 2024 to almost 14.5% per year in 2025."

A 400-basis point jump is a massive headwind for a fintech company that has to fund its credit portfolios and merchant prepayment business. Consequently, as stated in the Q4 earnings call, the financial costs rose 39% year-over-year, directly impacting margins. This macro situation is one reason I am changing my rating on PagSeguro. The second one is valuation.

Q4 Earnings Report

As in my previous article, I will use Brazilian reals to strip out currency volatility so we can better understand the fundamentals.

For FY 2025, net revenues reached BRL 13.4 billion, which represents a 15.5% YoY growth, before slowing down to a 12.4% YoY growth in Q4.

But we have to adjust the growth for inflation, which is currently around 4%. In this case, real growth is approx. 8%. This is much less than I estimated in my previous article, and thus my previously estimated potential upside is incorrect.

Now let’s analyze how revenues translated to the bottom line. Year-over-year earnings before tax expenses in Q4 2025 increased 12.7%, or 8.7% adjusting for inflation. This suggests the EBT margin remains in line.

Credit Portfolio Expansion

The credit portfolio could be the main future driver of revenue and margin growth. It is quite impressive that their core credit portfolio grew 32.8% YoY or 9.9% QoQ, especially considering the 14.5% interest rate environment, that I was talking about earlier. Currently, this high interest rate environment is a strong headwind for the company, and growing financial costs (up 39% YoY) serve as clear evidence. But I firmly believe such high interest rates will not last forever, and at some point, we will see Selic rates decrease. The headwind will then turn into a tailwind, providing a basis for margin expansion. When this happens, I believe, we will again see significant stock price appreciation.

The main problem is that we do not know when rates will be cut, and I am not in the guessing game. As such, this macro environment is one of the reasons for my Hold (Neutral) rating, which will turn bullish as soon as we see rates start to fall. Note that the Selic rate was cut 0.25% in March, but inflation in March also spiked from the lows in February, so I am unsure of further near-term rate reductions, especially due to the crude oil prices being on the rise. Until we see further evidence of lower rates, I remain neutral.

Stock Buybacks

As I anticipated in my last article, 2025 was a strong year for share buybacks. The company stated that ''from January 1, 2025, until December 31, 2025, we repurchased over 27.2 million shares.'' Note that these are impressive numbers, as there are currently 279 million shares outstanding, according to SA. The fact that they bought back approx. 10% of shares outstanding while still managing to grow 15.5% in 2025 is quite impressive, and it gives me a high degree of confidence in the management.

Valuation

PagSeguro is currently growing approx. 12.4% (or approx. 8% inflation adjusted), with EBT growing 12.7%, while trading at a 9x P/E multiple. Non-U.S. stocks are generally discounted, and as such, I believe we are now trading at a fair multiple. I expect that if we do not see any changes in the macro environment, specifically Selic rates, the stock should continue to trade at an 8-12x P/E multiple. But as stated above, once we see further interest rate cuts without the rising inflation, the story will change.

In my opinion, the margins should expand, since the cost of capital will decrease. At this point, EPS growth will reaccelerate, and the stock should continue its upward trajectory. But until then, I do not believe this stock can beat market returns, and an investor should reallocate capital to stocks with more upside potential.

Conclusion and Plan

I believe that currently, it is best to just avoid the stock or have a small tracking position. Should Selic rates meaningfully change, I will provide an update and potentially upgrade my PAGS rating.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: SCHO: Still A Bit Wary Of Duration
data.description:
The Schwab Short-Term US Treasury ETF (SCHO) is not that short duration. At 1.9 years it's still a bit sensitive to changes in YTMs around that horizon. Moreover, this exact horizon is where YTMs stand to change the most with the latest developments in the Middle East, specifically the ceasefire on all fronts and the return of parties to the negotiating table. Also, the Strait of Hormuz may see significantly more commercial traffic as of today's peace deal announcement. We think the end of the War hits at the heart of the Fed's rationale lately to keep rates where they are, whose decisions really matter for the SCHO as we covered previously. The end of the supply concerns and the headlines around the war could really affect consumer consciousness and resolve the dangers associated with a re-inflation. Therefore, short to intermediate term rates, around the 2 year mark for example, are the most liable to come down at this point. Given the fault lines exposed by the Iran War, we'd be less confident about betting on capital gains for longer duration as we believe there are still elevated structural inflation risks. The 2-year is a better mark for trying to play FI. But the USD is a risk for us as foreign investors, and the focus may go back to the debasement trade that has been wracking the USD for a year now.

SCHO Breakdown

The expense ratio is 0.03%, which is lower than some similar duration ETFs. The ETF is affected in terms of price effects by changes in benchmark rate assumptions, and very much by the Fed's policy stance in relation to factors currently visible and on the horizon.

Bull Case

The Fed made it clear that with a supply crisis whose scope and duration would be uncertain related to the Iran War and the closing of the Strait, markets might as well forget about cuts. Hence, the market fell, driven by high multiple tech names whose valuations rest substantially in the horizon value, subject most to compounding discounting effects from changes in levels of cost of capital.

SCHO, whose purposes is to be a low risk Treasury ETF, was a little wrong-footed by this and pricing effects therefore brought the investment into relatively poor performance territory YTD.

With ceasefires already here and peace talks underway, and with the Strait of Hormuz opened for the moment to commercial travel, YTM changes are coming in. With inflation risks less severe now, possibly with an end to the main barrier to the Fed's further rate cuts, 2 year Treasury rates are down 7.4 bps as of the time of this writing. A determined end would likely bring that down further as markets should anticipate that a rate cut path can be contemplated again, which is a relative advantage for FI investors looking to stick to Treasuries with the SCHO. The reason why rate cuts were unthinkable with the surge in oil prices is because of how front in mind gas prices are for US households, and how liable higher gas prices are to perpetuate higher inflation expectations that could bake into the economy through wage-price dynamics, perhaps only mitigated by the pretty weak labour market situation with the advent of AI.

Bear Case

The main aspect of the bear case is that for various unpredictable reasons, the conflict may end up reversing some of the benefits beginning to materialise.

But another issue that would be the case even if the War does end is that Treasuries are still USD denominated instruments, but USD-based investments are not necessarily the way to go when FX could become a significant performance detractor again. With yields coming down, the safe haven appeal of the USD falls again, and other negative market narratives could resume. For example we could see a return of the debasement trade. Moreover, the way the war played out may raise questions about US hegemony, where sensitivity to domestic electoral considerations seems to have been a factor for the push to end the War and avoid the economic pressures from higher prices.

Bottom Line

While 10 year rates are for the moment falling more than the 1 year rate, we are uncertain whether that should hold. Higher long term rate assumptions made some sense given the fault lines shown by the war: further fracturing between the US and its European and Gulf allies, apparent sensitivity to domestic electoral concerns, the relative success of the Iranian regime's survival against significant American and Israeli military intervention, Iran's powerful card of being able to interrupt the Strait as leverage point in possible future conflicts. We'd not want to bet on falling long-term YTMs. But at the 2-year mark, we think that a rate reduction path could resume )whether Powell leaves office or not) could be delayed pending Powell's investigation. The Fed independence issue is another reason to worry about long-term US rates, and folds into the debasement trade. The SCHO is lower expense ratio than other similar duration alternatives as well. The biggest concern we have is as foreign investors, we aren't sure we would want more USD exposure than necessary. For that reason we'd rather play the possibility of the end of the war coming with select equities instead.
